channel.title: NPR Topics: News
channel.description: NPR news, audio, and podcasts. Coverage of breaking stories, national and world news, politics, business, science, technology, and extended coverage of major national and world events.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Immigration board denies Mahmoud Khalil's appeal
item.description: The Board of Immigration Appeals has denied Mahmoud Khalil's latest attempt to dismiss his deportation case. This decision brings the Palestinian activist one step closer to possible expulsion.
item.content:encoded:
The Board of Immigration Appeals has denied Mahmoud Khalil's latest attempt to dismiss his deportation case. This decision brings the Palestinian activist one step closer to possible expulsion.

(Image credit: Matt Rourke)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel tells NBC News that he will not step down
item.description: Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel has told NBC News' Meet the Press that he would not step down in his first interview with a U.S. network.
item.content:encoded:
Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel has told NBC News' Meet the Press that he would not step down in his first interview with a U.S. network.

(Image credit: Adalberto Roque)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Melania Trump denies close ties to Jeffrey Epstein in rare public statement
item.description: The first lady made a rare public statement on Thursday saying she was not friends with the late sex offender. She also said Epstein did not introduce her to President Donald Trump.
item.content:encoded:
The first lady made a rare public statement on Thursday saying she was not friends with the late sex offender. She also said Epstein did not introduce her to President Donald Trump.

(Image credit: Samuel Corum)
item.dc:creator: Ava Berger

item.title: How an ancient resin traded for centuries got snarled up by the Iran war
item.description: It's not just energy supplies that have been disrupted by the Iran war. It's also hitting frankincense, a commodity that's been defining trade routes in the Middle East for thousands of years.
item.content:encoded:
It's not just energy supplies that have been disrupted by the Iran war. It's also hitting frankincense, a commodity that's been defining trade routes in the Middle East for thousands of years.

(Image credit: Maha Loubaris)
item.dc:creator: Scott Horsley

item.title: 710,000 fewer babies were born last year in U.S. compared with two decades ago
item.description: The U.S. fertility rate continued its slide to historic levels, due to plunging teen pregnancies and far more women delaying motherhood into their 30s and 40s.
item.content:encoded:
The U.S. fertility rate continued its slide to historic levels, due to plunging teen pregnancies and far more women delaying motherhood into their 30s and 40s.

(Image credit: Seth Wenig)
item.dc:creator: Brian Mann

item.title: How a cheese sandwich at the Masters is still $1.50 in an era of price hikes
item.description: The priciest concessions at the Masters, beer and wine, cost just $6 each. The Georgia golf tournament prides itself on a simple and affordable menu, even as ticket prices continue to climb.
item.content:encoded:
The priciest concessions at the Masters, beer and wine, cost just $6 each. The Georgia golf tournament prides itself on a simple and affordable menu, even as ticket prices continue to climb.

(Image credit: Andrew Redington)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: How bad for humans is wildlife trade? A new study has answers
item.description: People sell wild animals for food and for traditional medicine — legally and illegally. A study looks at the risks of spillover diseases from those pangolins, giant rats and other exotic critters.
item.content:encoded:
People sell wild animals for food and for traditional medicine — legally and illegally. A study looks at the risks of spillover diseases from those pangolins, giant rats and other exotic critters.

(Image credit: Jimin Lai/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: We can't all be astronauts, but the Artemis II crew has fitness lessons for everyone
item.description: The Artemis II astronauts don't have a lot of space to exercise. That's why they've got the flywheel — a small device that can be used for strength and cardio workouts.
item.content:encoded:
The Artemis II astronauts don't have a lot of space to exercise. That's why they've got the flywheel — a small device that can be used for strength and cardio workouts.

(Image credit: NASA)
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: This doctor turned a 31-foot RV into one of the country's only mobile OB-GYN clinics
item.description: Mary Fariba Afsari's book, Labor, is a portrait of reproductive healthcare in post-Dobbs America. Her book also is about her Iranian heritage and her grandmother's death from an illegal abortion.
item.content:encoded: Mary Fariba Afsari's book, Labor, is a portrait of reproductive healthcare in post-Dobbs America. Her book also is about her Iranian heritage and her grandmother's death from an illegal abortion.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: In the Trump era, everybody's talking about 'soft power.' But ... what is it exactly?
item.description: The U.S. government long saw giving international aid as a way to build goodwill throughout the world. Did it work? And what does the reducing of foreign aid mean for that effort now?
item.content:encoded:
The U.S. government long saw giving international aid as a way to build goodwill throughout the world. Did it work? And what does the reducing of foreign aid mean for that effort now?

(Image credit: Ben de la Cruz/NPR)
item.dc:creator: Fatma Tanis
